info.title: Dio findings: OTP, race, stuck events

parts.text:
• Findings

  - High: web/server/api/dio/recover.ts:34 creates an OTP but never sends it. The UI says “we’ll email you a verification code” at web/src/components/DioAccessPromptDialog.tsx:248, but there is no mail provider, log-only dev
  fallback, or returned dev code. Recovery is now safer than before, but non-functional for real users.
  - High: web/src/dio-provider/server.ts:297 still does a non-atomic getItem then setItem event lock. Concurrent duplicate webhooks can both acquire the lock and create multiple Bifrost keys. The previous race concern is not
  actually fixed unless Nitro storage provides atomic compare-and-set here, which this code does not use.
  - Medium: web/server/api/dio/webhook.ts:57 can leave events stuck in processing forever if the process dies after lock acquisition and before completeEvent. Future Creem retries will be treated as duplicates at web/src/dio-
  provider/server.ts:305. Add a processing TTL/stale-lock recovery.
  - Medium: If Bifrost key creation succeeds but web/server/api/dio/webhook.ts:115 fails, retrying a failed event can create another virtual key because idempotency is only by stored entitlement/email after persistence. Use a
  purchase-reference idempotency key with Bifrost or store a provisioning record before/around key creation.

This is a code review finding list. The user hasn't explicitly asked me to fix these, but the context is a coding task. I should probably implement the fixes. Let me first explore the relevant files to understand the codebase, then make the necessary changes.

Let me read the files mentioned:
1. web/server/api/dio/recover.ts
2. web/src/components/DioAccessPromptDialog.tsx
3. web/src/dio-provider/server.ts
4. web/server/api/dio/webhook.ts

Let me start by reading these files and understanding the dio-provider structure.
I’ll read the affected Dio files, then fix the four findings.

1: import { createError, defineEventHandler, readBody } from "h3";
2: import { useStorage } from "nitro/storage";
3: import {
4: 	createRecoveryChallenge,
5: 	loadDioEntitlement,
6: 	normalizeEmail,
7: 	verifyRecoveryChallenge,
8: } from "../../../src/dio-provider/server";
9: 
10: interface RecoverBody {
11: 	email?: string;
12: 	otp?: string;
13: }
14: 
15: export default defineEventHandler(async (event) => {
16: 	if (event.method !== "POST") {
17: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 405, statusMessage: "Method not allowed" });
18: 	}
19: 
20: 	const body = await readBody<RecoverBody>(event);
21: 	const email = normalizeEmail(body?.email ?? "");
22: 	if (!email || !/^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email)) {
23: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 400, statusMessage: "Valid email is required" });
24: 	}
25: 
26: 	const storage = useStorage("keating:dio");
27: 	const entitlement = await loadDioEntitlement(storage, email);
28: 	if (!entitlement) {
29: 		return { success: false, pending: false };
30: 	}
31: 
32: 	// Recovery requires a one-time code to prove email ownership. The code is
33: 	// generated on the first request and "sent" to the email address.
34: 	if (!body?.otp) {
35: 		await createRecoveryChallenge(storage, email);
36: 		return { success: false, requiresOtp: true };
37: 	}
38: 
39: 	const valid = await verifyRecoveryChallenge(storage, email, body.otp);
40: 	if (!valid) {
41: 		return { success: false, requiresOtp: true, error: "Invalid or expired verification code" };
42: 	}
43: 
44: 	return {
45: 		success: true,
46: 		apiKey: entitlement.virtualKeyValue,
47: 	};
48: });

(End of file - total 48 lines)
</content>

116: export function parseCreemWebhookBody(body: unknown): CreemWebhookEvent {
117: 	if (!body || typeof body !== "object") {
118: 		throw new Error("Invalid webhook payload");
119: 	}
120: 	const payload = body as Record<string, unknown>;
121: 	if (typeof payload.id !== "string" || !payload.id) {

123: 	}
124: 	if (typeof payload.eventType !== "string" || !payload.eventType) {

131: }

133: export function verifyCreemWebhookSignature(body: string, signature: string, secret: string): boolean {
134: 	if (!signature) return false;
135: 	const computed = createHmac("sha256", secret).update(body).digest("hex");
136: 	if (computed.length !== signature.length) return false;
137: 	try {
138: 		const computedBuf = Buffer.from(computed, "hex");
139: 		const signatureBuf = Buffer.from(signature, "hex");
140: 		return timingSafeEqual(computedBuf, signatureBuf);
141: 	} catch {
142: 		return false;
143: 	}
144: }

160: 	config: DioEnvConfig,
161: 	request: BifrostVirtualKeyRequest,

163: 	const url = `${config.bifrostBaseUrl.replace(/\/+$/, "")}/api/governance/virtual-keys`;
164: 	const response = await fetch(url, {
165: 		method: "POST",
166: 		headers: {

169: 		},
170: 		body: JSON.stringify({
171: 			name: request.name,
172: 			description: request.description,
173: 			allowed_models: request.allowedModels,
174: 			budget: request.budget,
175: 			metadata: request.metadata,

177: 	});
178: 
179: 	const body = await response.json().catch(() => ({}));
180: 	if (!response.ok) {
181: 		const message = body?.message || body?.error || `Bifrost virtual key creation failed (${response.status})`;
182: 		throw new Error(message);
183: 	}
184: 
185: 	const virtualKey = body?.virtual_key || body;
186: 	if (typeof virtualKey?.id !== "string" || typeof virtualKey?.value !== "string") {
187: 		throw new Error("Bifrost virtual key response missing id or value");
188: 	}
189: 
190: 	return { id: virtualKey.id, value: virtualKey.value };
191: }

193: export interface DioEntitlement {
194: 	email: string;
195: 	virtualKeyId: string;
196: 	virtualKeyValue: string;
197: 	purchaseReference?: string;
198: 	orderId?: string;
199: 	checkoutId?: string;
200: 	productId?: string;
201: 	customerId?: string;
202: 	createdAt: string;
203: }
204: 
205: const ENTITLEMENT_KEY_PREFIX = "entitlement:";
206: const EVENT_KEY_PREFIX = "event:";
207: const RECOVERY_KEY_PREFIX = "recovery:";
208: 
209: export interface DioEventRecord {
210: 	status: "processing" | "succeeded" | "failed";
211: 	createdAt: string;
212: 	error?: string;
213: }
214: 
215: export interface RecoveryChallenge {
216: 	email: string;
217: 	otpHash: string;
218: 	expiresAt: string;
219: 	attempts: number;
220: }
221: 
222: export function entitlementStorageKey(email: string): string {
223: 	return `${ENTITLEMENT_KEY_PREFIX}${normalizeEmail(email)}`;
224: }
225: 
226: function eventStorageKey(eventId: string): string {
227: 	return `${EVENT_KEY_PREFIX}${eventId}`;
228: }
229: 
230: function recoveryStorageKey(email: string): string {
231: 	return `${RECOVERY_KEY_PREFIX}${normalizeEmail(email)}`;
232: }
233: 
234: function hashOtp(otp: string): string {
235: 	return createHash("sha256").update(otp).digest("hex");
236: }
237: 
238: function generateOtp(): string {
239: 	return Array.from({ length: 6 }, () => Math.floor(Math.random() * 10)).join("");
240: }
241: 
242: export async function createRecoveryChallenge(
243: 	storage: { setItem: (key: string, value: unknown) => Promise<void> },
244: 	email: string,
245: ): Promise<string> {
246: 	const otp = generateOtp();
247: 	const challenge: RecoveryChallenge = {
248: 		email,
249: 		otpHash: hashOtp(otp),
250: 		expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 15 * 60 * 1000).toISOString(),
251: 		attempts: 0,
252: 	};
253: 	await storage.setItem(recoveryStorageKey(email), challenge);
254: 	return otp;
255: }
256: 
257: export async function verifyRecoveryChallenge(
258: 	storage: { getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>; setItem: (key: string, value: unknown) => Promise<void> },
259: 	email: string,
260: 	otp: string,
261: ): Promise<boolean> {
262: 	const key = recoveryStorageKey(email);
263: 	const value = await storage.getItem(key);
264: 	if (!value || typeof value !== "object") {
265: 		return false;
266: 	}
267: 	const challenge = value as RecoveryChallenge;
268: 	if (challenge.attempts >= 5) {
269: 		return false;
270: 	}
271: 	if (new Date(challenge.expiresAt) < new Date()) {
272: 		await storage.setItem(key, { ...challenge, attempts: challenge.attempts + 1 });
273: 		return false;
274: 	}
275: 	const expectedHash = Buffer.from(challenge.otpHash, "hex");
276: 	const actualHash = Buffer.from(hashOtp(otp), "hex");
277: 	if (expectedHash.length !== actualHash.length) {
278: 		await storage.setItem(key, { ...challenge, attempts: challenge.attempts + 1 });
279: 		return false;
280: 	}
281: 	const ok = timingSafeEqual(expectedHash, actualHash);
282: 	if (!ok) {
283: 		await storage.setItem(key, { ...challenge, attempts: challenge.attempts + 1 });
284: 		return false;
285: 	}
286: 	await storage.setItem(key, { ...challenge, attempts: challenge.attempts + 1 });
287: 	return true;
288: }
289: 
290: /**
291:  * Acquire a per-event processing lock using a dedicated storage key.
292:  *
293:  * Returns false if the event is already processing or has succeeded, which
294:  * prevents duplicate Creem webhooks from racing. A previously "failed" event
295:  * can be retried.
296:  */
297: export async function acquireEventLock(
298: 	storage: { getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>; setItem: (key: string, value: unknown) => Promise<void> },
299: 	eventId: string,
300: ): Promise<boolean> {
301: 	const key = eventStorageKey(eventId);
302: 	const existing = await storage.getItem(key);
303: 	if (existing && typeof existing === "object") {
304: 		const record = existing as DioEventRecord;
305: 		if (record.status === "processing" || record.status === "succeeded") {
306: 			return false;
307: 		}
308: 	}
309: 	const record: DioEventRecord = { status: "processing", createdAt: new Date().toISOString() };
310: 	await storage.setItem(key, record);
311: 	return true;
312: }
313: 
314: export async function completeEvent(
315: 	storage: { setItem: (key: string, value: unknown) => Promise<void> },
316: 	eventId: string,
317: 	succeeded: boolean,
318: 	error?: string,
319: ): Promise<void> {
320: 	const key = eventStorageKey(eventId);
321: 	const record: DioEventRecord = {
322: 		status: succeeded ? "succeeded" : "failed",
323: 		createdAt: new Date().toISOString(),
324: 		error,
325: 	};
326: 	await storage.setItem(key, record);
327: }
328: 
329: /** @deprecated Prefer {@link acquireEventLock} and {@link completeEvent}. */
330: export async function markEventProcessed(
331: 	storage: { getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>; setItem: (key: string, value: unknown) => Promise<void> },
332: 	eventId: string,
333: ): Promise<boolean> {
334: 	const locked = await acquireEventLock(storage, eventId);
335: 	if (!locked) return false;
336: 	await completeEvent(storage, eventId, true);
337: 	return true;
338: }
339: 
340: export async function loadDioEntitlement(
341: 	storage: { getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown> },
342: 	email: string,
343: ): Promise<DioEntitlement | null> {
344: 	const value = await storage.getItem(entitlementStorageKey(email));
345: 	if (value && typeof value === "object") {
346: 		const entitlement = value as DioEntitlement;
347: 		if (
348: 			typeof entitlement.email === "string" &&
349: 			typeof entitlement.virtualKeyId === "string" &&
350: 			typeof entitlement.virtualKeyValue === "string"
351: 		) {
352: 			return entitlement;
353: 		}
354: 	}
355: 	return null;
356: }
357: 
358: export async function saveDioEntitlement(
359: 	storage: { setItem: (key: string, value: unknown) => Promise<void> },
360: 	entitlement: DioEntitlement,
361: ): Promise<void> {
362: 	await storage.setItem(entitlementStorageKey(entitlement.email), entitlement);
363: }
364: 
365: function isValidEmail(email: string): boolean {
366: 	return /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email);
367: }
368: 
369: function cryptoUUID(): string {
370: 	if (typeof crypto !== "undefined" && typeof crypto.randomUUID === "function") {
371: 		return crypto.randomUUID();
372: 	}
373: 	const bytes = new Uint8Array(16);
374: 	if (typeof crypto !== "undefined" && typeof crypto.getRandomValues === "function") {
375: 		crypto.getRandomValues(bytes);
376: 	} else {
377: 		for (let i = 0; i < bytes.length; i++) {
378: 			bytes[i] = Math.floor(Math.random() * 256);
379: 		}
380: 	}
381: 	bytes[6] = (bytes[6] & 0x0f) | 0x40;
382: 	bytes[8] = (bytes[8] & 0x3f) | 0x80;
383: 	const hex = Array.from(bytes, (b) => b.toString(16).padStart(2, "0")).join("");
384: 	return `${hex.slice(0, 8)}-${hex.slice(8, 12)}-${hex.slice(12, 16)}-${hex.slice(16, 20)}-${hex.slice(20)}`;
385: }

1: import { createError, defineEventHandler, getHeader, readRawBody } from "h3";
2: import { useStorage } from "nitro/storage";
3: import {
4: 	acquireEventLock,
5: 	completeEvent,
6: 	createBifrostVirtualKey,
7: 	getDioEnvConfig,
8: 	loadDioEntitlement,
9: 	parseCreemWebhookBody,
10: 	saveDioEntitlement,
11: 	verifyCreemWebhookSignature,
12: 	type DioEntitlement,
13: } from "../../../src/dio-provider/server";
14: 
15: export default defineEventHandler(async (event) => {
16: 	if (event.method !== "POST") {
17: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 405, statusMessage: "Method not allowed" });
18: 	}
19: 
20: 	const config = getDioEnvConfig();
21: 	const bodyString = (await readRawBody(event)) ?? "";
22: 	const signature = getHeader(event, "creem-signature") ?? "";
23: 
24: 	if (!verifyCreemWebhookSignature(bodyString, signature, config.creemWebhookSecret)) {
25: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 401, statusMessage: "Invalid webhook signature" });
26: 	}
27: 
28: 	let payload: ReturnType<typeof parseCreemWebhookBody>;
29: 	try {
30: 		payload = parseCreemWebhookBody(JSON.parse(bodyString));
31: 	} catch (err) {
32: 		throw createError({
33: 			statusCode: 400,
34: 			statusMessage: err instanceof Error ? err.message : "Invalid webhook payload",
35: 		});
36: 	}
37: 
38: 	if (payload.eventType !== "checkout.completed") {
39: 		return { received: true, handled: false };
40: 	}
41: 
42: 	const storage = useStorage("keating:dio");
43: 	const object = payload.object;
44: 	const productId = object?.order?.product || object?.product?.id;
45: 	if (productId !== config.creemProductId) {
46: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 400, statusMessage: "Product ID mismatch" });
47: 	}
48: 
49: 	const customerEmail = object?.customer?.email;
50: 	if (!customerEmail) {
51: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 400, statusMessage: "Missing customer email" });
52: 	}
53: 	const email = customerEmail.toLowerCase();
54: 
55: 	// Acquire a per-event lock before any side effects. This prevents duplicate
56: 	// Creem webhooks from racing to create multiple Bifrost virtual keys.
57: 	const locked = await acquireEventLock(storage, payload.id);
58: 	if (!locked) {
59: 		const existing = await loadDioEntitlement(storage, email);
60: 		return {
61: 			received: true,
62: 			handled: false,
63: 			reason: "duplicate event",
64: 			entitlement: existing ? { email, virtualKeyId: existing.virtualKeyId } : undefined,
65: 		};
66: 	}
67: 
68: 	try {
69: 		// Idempotency by email: if an entitlement already exists, do not create a
70: 		// second virtual key.
71: 		const existing = await loadDioEntitlement(storage, email);
72: 		if (existing) {
73: 			await completeEvent(storage, payload.id, true);
74: 			return { received: true, handled: true, entitlement: { email, virtualKeyId: existing.virtualKeyId } };
75: 		}
76: 
77: 		const purchaseReference =
78: 			(object?.request_id as string | undefined) ||
79: 			(typeof object?.metadata?.dio_purchase_reference === "string"
80: 				? object.metadata.dio_purchase_reference
81: 				: undefined) ||
82: 			payload.id;
83: 
84: 		const virtualKey = await createBifrostVirtualKey(config, {
85: 			name: `keating:${email}:${purchaseReference}`,
86: 			description: `Keating Dio credits for ${email}. Creem checkout ${object?.id ?? "unknown"}, order ${object?.order?.id ?? "unknown"}`,
87: 			allowedModels: [config.bifrostModelAlias],
88: 			budget: { max: config.dioCreditBudget },
89: 			metadata: {
90: 				email,
91: 				purchaseReference,
92: 				creemEventId: payload.id,
93: 				creemCheckoutId: object?.id,
94: 				creemOrderId: object?.order?.id,
95: 				creemProductId: productId,
96: 				creemCustomerId: object?.customer?.id,
97: 				source: "creem-webhook",
98: 			},
99: 		});
100: 
101: 		const entitlement: DioEntitlement = {
102: 			email,
103: 			virtualKeyId: virtualKey.id,
104: 			virtualKeyValue: virtualKey.value,
105: 			purchaseReference,
106: 			orderId: object?.order?.id,
107: 			checkoutId: object?.id,
108: 			productId,
109: 			customerId: object?.customer?.id,
110: 			createdAt: new Date().toISOString(),
111: 		};
112: 
113: 		// Only mark the event as succeeded after the entitlement is persisted.
114: 		// If persistence fails, Creem retries will reprocess the event.
115: 		await saveDioEntitlement(storage, entitlement);
116: 		await completeEvent(storage, payload.id, true);
117: 
118: 		return { received: true, handled: true, entitlement: { email, virtualKeyId: virtualKey.id } };
119: 	} catch (err) {
120: 		const message = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
121: 		await completeEvent(storage, payload.id, false, message);
122: 		throw createError({ statusCode: 500, statusMessage: message });
123: 	}
124: });

(End of file - total 124 lines)
</content>

parts.text:
Now I understand the code. Let me plan the fixes.

## Findings:

### 1. High: OTP created but never sent
`web/server/api/dio/recover.ts:34` creates an OTP but never sends it. The UI says “we’ll email you a verification code” but there is no mail provider, log-only dev fallback, or returned dev code.

Fix:
- Add an email transport abstraction in `dio-provider/server.ts` 
- In dev/test environments, log the OTP to console and optionally return it in response when `NODE_ENV === "development"` or some `DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE` flag
- In production, use SMTP or an email provider. Keep it simple: add optional SMTP config env vars and a `sendEmail` function that uses nodemailer or a simple SMTP fetch. Actually, to avoid adding Heavy dependency, maybe use Resend API or SMTP via fetch.
- Since this is a minimal fix, I'll add:
  - `DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE=true` returns the OTP in API response for dev
  - `DIO_RECOVERY_SMTP_URL` / `DIO_RECOVERY_FROM` for SMTP transport using built-in Node.js `net`? Too complex.
  - Simplest robust approach: use `fetch` to call a generic email provider endpoint like Resend or Mailgun if configured, otherwise log and (in dev) return.
  - Actually, a minimal generic approach: add `DIO_RECOVERY_PROVIDER=resend|smtp|logger` but that adds deps.
  - Looking at project: uses Bun, Node 22. We could use `nodemailer` but it's not in deps. Better to add no new deps and use a simple SMTP submission via `fetch` to Resend API if `RESEND_API_KEY` is set.
  - Or even simpler: add `sendRecoveryEmail(config, email, otp)` function that tries providers in order:
    1. If `RESEND_API_KEY` + `DIO_RECOVERY_FROM` set, send via Resend API using fetch
    2. If dev (`NODE_ENV=development` or `DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE=1`), log OTP and return it
    3. Otherwise log warning that email not sent and return `sent: false`
  - In recover.ts, call send email. If dev code mode, return `{ success: false, requiresOtp: true, devCode?: string }`.
  - Update UI to show dev code if returned? No, the finding says "returned dev code" is one option. So add `devCode` to response in dev mode.

Actually the finding says: "there is no mail provider, log-only dev fallback, or returned dev code". So I should add at least one of these. I'll add:
- Resend API support (mail provider)
- Console log dev fallback with returned dev code in dev mode

### 2. High: Non-atomic event lock
`web/src/dio-provider/server.ts:297` still does a non-atomic getItem then setItem event lock. Concurrent duplicate webhooks can both acquire the lock.

Fix:
- Need atomic compare-and-set. Nitro storage is unjs storage. Most drivers support `setItem` but not atomic CAS. However, Nitro storage has `setItemRaw` and some drivers support atomic operations? Let me check.
- Actually, unjs storage has `setItem(key, value, opts)`? I think the latest unjs/ipx has `overwrite: false` option maybe? Let me think...
- In Nitro, `useStorage` returns unjs/unstorage instance. unstorage has `setItem(key, value, options)` where options may include `nx: true` for "only if not exists" (Redis style). Actually unstorage has `setItem(key, value, options?: CreateOptions)` and `createOptions` includes `nx` and `xx`. Let me verify.
- I recall unstorage has `setItem(key, value, opts: { nx?: boolean; xx?: boolean })` where nx = only if not exists, xx = only if exists. This is supported by some drivers.
- If supported, we can do atomic set with `nx: true`. But if the driver doesn't support it, it would just overwrite. 
- However, for the standard file system driver (default in Nitro), unstorage might not support nx. Actually, now that I think, unstorage has a `setItem` with `DriverSetOptions` that includes `nx` since v1.13 or so. But is it truly atomic on filesystem? Probably not.
- The finding states "unless Nitro storage provides atomic compare-and-set here, which this code does not use". So I should use atomic CAS if available.
- A more robust approach: implement a small atomic lock using file system advisory locking if on Node.js. But we don't know the storage driver.
- Actually, the recommended fix is to use the storage driver's atomic capabilities. Use `storage.setItem(key, value, { nx: true })` and catch errors, or use a separate "lock" key.
- Let me check if Nitro/unstorage has `setItem` with `{ nx: true }`.

I can test by fetching unstorage types from node_modules.

Index: AGENTS.md
===================================================================
--- AGENTS.md	
+++ AGENTS.md	
@@ -1,262 +1,267 @@
 # AGENTS.md — Keating
 
 > What future agents need to know to work effectively in this repo.
 
 ## Overview
 
 Keating is a Pi-powered "hyperteacher" — a CLI tool + web app that generates pedagogical artifacts (lesson plans, maps, animations, benchmarks, policy evolution) deterministically from a local Node.js core, while the actual interactive teaching experience lives in Pi prompt/skill/extension templates. The design deliberately separates: (1) the interactive Pi shell runtime layer and (2) the deterministic pedagogy engine.
 
 ## Project Structure
 
 - **`src/core/`** — Deterministic pedagogy engine. All local, testable, no LLM calls except `animation.ts` (scene generation) and `pi-agent.ts` (thin LLM wrapper).
 - **`src/cli/`** — Terminal CLI entrypoint (`main.ts`) and interactive setup (`setup.ts`).
 - **`src/runtime/`** — Pi/Feynman runtime detection and shell launcher.
 - **`src/pi/`** — Pi extension entrypoint (`hyperteacher-extension.ts`). Registers `/plan`, `/map`, `/animate`, `/bench`, `/evolve`, `/prompt-evolve`, `/feedback`, `/policy`, `/outputs`, `/trace`, etc.
 - **`pi/prompts/`** — Teaching prompt templates (`bridge.md`, `diagnose.md`, `improve.md`, `learn.md`, `quiz.md`).
 - **`pi/skills/`** — Pi skills directory for the runtime.
 - **`web/`** — Browser UI (React + TanStack Router + Vite + Nitro). Separate `package.json`, separate build.
 - **`test/`** — Root-level test suite. **Not** in `web/test/`, which is for web-specific tests.
 - **`scripts/`** — Build/utility scripts, plus `install/install.sh` used in release bundles.
 - **`docs/`** — Architecture docs, Typst study paper, VHS tape scripts for recordings.
 - **`video/`** — Remotion video project (`keating-intro/`).
 - **`bin/keating.js`** — Executable entrypoint for the CLI.
 
 ## Build & Test Commands
 
 This project uses **Bun** as its runtime. Do not assume npm/pnpm.
 All dev dependencies (bun, node, oxdraw, typst, similarity, just, etc.) are managed by **Flox**. Run `flox activate` to enter the dev environment.
 Task runner is **just** — run `just` to list available tasks.
 
 | Task | Command |
 |------|---------|
 | Install deps | `just install` (root + web; auto-runs on `flox activate` if missing) |
 | Build root | `just build` — compiles to `dist/` via `tsc` with NodeNext resolution |
 | Build everything | `just build-all` — root + web (vite + nitro) |
 | Test root | `just test` — uses `bun:test` runtime with `fast-check` for property-based testing |
 | Test web | `just test-web` |
 | Mutation testing | `just mutate` — Stryker command runner against `src/core/` |
 | Run CLI | `bun src/cli/main.ts <command>` or `node ./bin/keating.js <command>` |
 | Dev server (web) | `just web` (Vite dev on port 3000) |
 | Web build | `just web-build` — `vite build && nitro build`, outputs to `web/dist/` and `web/.output/` |
 | Web preview | `just web-preview` |
 | Render docs diagrams | `just docs-diagrams` |
 | Render intro video | `just video-intro` |
 | Check versions | `just check-version` — CI-friendly read-only version sync check |
 | Sync versions | `just sync-version` — auto-fix all tracked version strings |
 
 **Just tasks** (`justfile`) are the canonical dev workflow: `just build`, `just test`, `just shell`, `just doctor`, `just bench`, `just evolve`, `just prompt-evolve`, `just map <topic>`, `just animate <topic>`, `just trace`, etc. Run `just` with no arguments to see all available tasks.
 
 ## Code Organization & Architecture
 
 ### Three-Layer Split
 
 1. **Pi Runtime Layer** — `pi/prompts/`, `pi/skills/`, `src/pi/hyperteacher-extension.ts`. Flexible, interactive teaching shell. The extension registers slash commands (`/plan`, `/map`, etc.) that delegate to `src/core/project.ts`.
 2. **Deterministic Pedagogy Layer** — `src/core/lesson-plan.ts`, `src/core/map.ts`, `src/core/animation.ts`, `src/core/verification.ts`, `src/core/benchmark.ts`, `src/core/evolution.ts`, `src/core/prompt-evolution.ts`, `src/core/map-elites.ts`, etc. All pure/local logic. Produces inspectable artifacts under `.keating/outputs/`.
 
 ### Deterministic vs Non-Deterministic Boundary
 
 Everything in `src/core/` is deterministic **except**:
 - `animation.ts` — calls `piComplete()` (LLM) to generate `manim-web` scene JavaScript. Falls back to a basic stub if the LLM call fails.
 - `pi-agent.ts` — thin wrapper over the Pi AI runtime for completions.
 
 All topic definitions, lesson plans, benchmarks, policy mutations, and prompt scoring are deterministic — this makes the test suite fast and LLM-independent.
 
 ### Key Data Flows
 
 - **CLI commands** (`src/cli/main.ts`) → `src/core/project.ts` artifact functions → write to `.keating/outputs/`
 - **Pi extension** (`src/pi/hyperteacher-extension.ts`) → same `project.ts` functions but wired to `ctx.ui.setEditorText()`, `ctx.ui.notify()`
 - **Web** (`web/src/hooks/useKeatingAgent.tsx`) → `@mariozechner/pi-agent-core` Agent → `web/src/keating/browser-tools.ts` (tool definitions) → tool execution
 - **Synthetic learner simulation** (`helpers.ts` stub) → deterministic score based on policy/weights → no real LLM in tests
 
 ### Browser Port
 
 `web/src/keating/core.ts` is a hand-port of `src/core/types.ts` for the browser (no Node.js fs imports). `web/src/keating/browser-tools.ts` re-implements CLI tools for the web agent. When updating core types, check if the browser port needs updating too.
 
 ### Artifact Directory Layout
 
 Artifacts are written under `.keating/` in the current working directory (gitignored). Structure:
 ```
 .keating/
   plans/<topic>.md
   maps/<topic>.mmd          (+ .svg if oxdraw available)
   outputs/animations/<topic>/player.html, scene.mjs, storyboard.md, manifest.json
   outputs/benchmarks/<topic>-
   outputs/evolution/
   outputs/prompt-evolution/
   outputs/traces/
   outputs/verifications/
   state/learner.json
   policy/
 ```
 
 **Important**: `keating.config.json` and `.keating/` are per-project, gitignored. They are NOT checked into this repo. The checked-in `keating.config.json` in the repo root is only an example.
 
 ## TypeScript Conventions
 
 - **Module resolution**: `NodeNext` at root, `Bundler` in web. All root imports use `.js` extensions (e.g., `import { foo } from "./bar.js"`).
 - **Strict mode**: enabled. No implicit any.
 - **Root tsconfig**: `src/**/*.ts`, `outDir: "dist"`, `rootDir: "."`. Compiles `src/` into `dist/src/`.
 - **Web tsconfig**: separate in `web/`, uses `@/` alias → `web/src/`.
 - **React**: version 19. Web uses Tailwind CSS v4 with `@tailwindcss/vite`.
 
 ## Key Patterns & Gotchas
 
 ### Import Extensions
 Root `src/` files **must** use `.js` extensions in imports, even for `.ts` files. The web package does not.
 
 ### Topic Resolution & Fallbacks
 `resolveTopic()` in `src/core/topics.ts` looks up a hardcoded `TOPICS` record. If a topic is not found, `buildFallbackTopic()` auto-generates one using domain keyword heuristics and domain-specific example/exercise templates. This means giving an unknown topic string still produces a valid lesson plan — but quality depends on the domain guess.
 
 ### Domain-Specific Phase Injections
 `buildLessonPlan()` in `src/core/lesson-plan.ts` injects extra phases based on `topic.domain`:
 - `code` → inserts "Live Code" phase after examples
 - `law` → appends citation instructions to examples
 - `medicine` → appends evidence-level references to formal core
 - `history` → appends timeline/source instructions to examples
 - `psychology` → appends replication status flags to misconceptions
 - `politics` → appends competing frameworks to transfer
 - `arts` → appends specific work analysis to examples
 
 ### Policy Clamping
 All `TeacherPolicy` scalars must stay in `[0, 1]`. `clampPolicy()` in `src/core/policy.ts` enforces this. `exerciseCount` is clamped to integer `[1, 5]`. Weights are normalized to sum to 1. Tests assert these invariants.
 
 ### Animation Scene Generation
 `animationSceneSource()` calls `piComplete()` with a detailed prompt asking for raw JS (no markdown blocks). The response is stripped of ` ```js ` fences. If the call throws, it falls back to a minimal stub. The scene kind is selected by domain (math → function-graph, science → distribution-bars, code → code-trace, etc.).
 
 ### WeakSet for Speech Tool Deduplication
 `registerSpeechTool()` in `src/pi/hyperteacher-extension.ts` uses a `WeakSet<object>` to prevent double-registering the `keating_voice` tool on the same Pi instance across multiple session starts.
 
 ### Web Proxy Plugin
 `web/vite.config.ts` contains a `chatProxyPlugin()` that proxies `/api/chat-proxy/**` to the target API specified in the `x-target-url` header. This is for browser CORS workaround when calling external LLM APIs. In production (Nitro), this is handled by `server/api/chat-proxy/[...slug].ts`.
 
 ### Web PWA
 The web app is a PWA via `vite-plugin-pwa`. It caches WASM/ONNX files and HuggingFace model downloads with long expiration. WebGPU models are loaded via `@huggingface/transformers` — excluded from `optimizeDeps`. COOP/COEP headers are set in Vite dev server for SharedArrayBuffer support.
 
 ### Nitro Routing Gotchas
 `web/nitro.config.ts` sets `fallthrough: false` for all static asset extensions so missing files return 404 instead of falling back to `index.html` (SPA route). The catch-all `/**: { static: true }` serves the SPA for unmatched routes.
 
 ### Release Bundle
 The release workflow bundles `node_modules` into the tarball for a truly standalone install. The install script (`scripts/install/install.sh`) is moved to the bundle root, and the `install/` directory is deleted during packaging.
 
 ### Node Version
 `package.json` specifies `engines: { "node": ">=20.19.0" }`. Bun is the primary runtime used in CI.
 
 ## Testing Strategy
 
 Follows an **Antithesis-style** mindset: semantic system laws over brittle snapshots.
 
 - **Property tests** (`fast-check`): randomized policies and topics should always produce coherent lesson plans, bounded scores, valid policy fields.
 - **Fuzz tests**: random topic strings, random policies, repeated map generation should not crash.
 - **Acceptance tests**: full artifact pipeline in a temp directory — verify plans, maps, benchmark reports, evolution reports, prompt-evolution artifacts, traces, and policy state are all created.
 - **Deterministic stubs**: `test/helpers.ts` provides `stubBenchmarkResult()` and `createDeterministicBenchmark()` so the benchmark/evolution code is testable without an LLM.
 - **Invariant helpers**: `policyIsBounded()`, `weightsAreNormalized()`, `benchmarkScoresAreBounded()` are used in tests to assert system laws continuously.
 
 Tests use `bun:test` (not vitest/jest). Run with `bun test ./test/*.test.ts`.
 
 ## Prompt Evolution
 
 `prompt-evolve` scores prompt templates on 6 objectives: `voice`, `diagnosis`, `verification`, `retrieval`, `transfer`, `structure`. It does **not** silently overwrite the source prompt. It writes:
 - `.keating/outputs/prompt-evolution/<name>.md` (report)
 - `.keating/outputs/prompt-evolution/<name>.evolved.md` (evolved snapshot)
 
 Successive runs resume from the prior `*.evolved.md` artifact when available, so evolution accumulates across runs rather than always restarting from the base prompt.
 
 The selector is PROSPER-style: balanced multi-objective candidates beat narrow overfit edits.
 
 ## Flox Environment
 
 All system-level dev dependencies are managed by Flox (`.flox/env/manifest.toml`):
 - **bun** — JS runtime, bundler, package manager
 - **nodejs_22** — Node.js for tools that need it
 - **oxdraw** — diagram rendering (Mermaid → SVG, Linux only)
 - **typst** — Typesetting for doc generation
 - **imagemagick** — image processing for diagram export
 - **similarity** — code similarity detection (`similarity-ts`)
 - **just** — task runner (replaces mise)
 - **gh** — GitHub CLI
 - **ripgrep**, **fd** — fast search utilities
 
 Run `flox activate` to enter the dev environment. The activation hook auto-installs `node_modules` if missing. An `alias k="bun src/cli/main.ts"` is provided for quick CLI access.
 
 ## Speech Module
 
 Disabled by default. When `speech.enabled: true` in `keating.config.json`, a `keating_voice` tool is registered. In the Pi shell, it emits structured voice-tagged utterances (not audio), keeping the speech loop optional. In the web app, the same tool routes through Gemini Flash Live with actual audio output. The web app speech toggle is independent of CLI config.
 
 ## Commands Summary
 
 ```bash
 # Core CLI commands
 keating shell              # Launch Pi shell
 keating setup [--yes]      # Interactive config setup
 keating doctor             # Runtime diagnostic report
 keating version            # Show current version
 keating web [port]         # Start local web server
 
 # Artifact generation
 keating plan <topic>
 keating map <topic>
 keating animate <topic>
 keating verify <topic>
 
 # Evaluation & improvement
 keating bench [topic]           # Benchmark current policy (weights derived from learner feedback)
 keating evolve [topic]          # Policy evolution with MAP-Elites (grids persist between runs)
 keating prompt-evolve [prompt]  # Prompt evolution (default: "learn")
 keating auto-improve [topic]    # Full loop: bench → evolve → prompt-evolve → bench (30-min cooldown, auto-rollback on regression)
 keating auto-improve [topic] --force  # Override cooldown
 keating improve                 # Self-improvement proposal from benchmark weaknesses
 keating improve accept <id>     # Accept a pending improvement proposal
 keating improve reject <id>     # Reject a pending proposal and restore snapshots
 keating edit <file>             # Apply search/replace edit (stdin JSON or interactive mode)
 
 # State inspection
 keating policy             # Show active policy
 keating trace [substring]  # Browse artifact traces
 keating learner-state      # Learner profile
 keating timeline           # Engagement timeline (spaced repetition)
 keating due                # Topics due for review
 keating feedback up|down|confused [topic] [--comment=...]
 ```
 
 ## Environment Variables
 
 - `GEMINI_API_KEY`
 - `OPENAI_API_KEY`
 - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
 
 Keating tries the configured provider first, then falls back to OpenAI/Anthropic if the default key is missing.
 
 ### Web / Dio provider
 
 Used by the web app's Dio credit purchase and Bifrost virtual-key provisioning routes. Never expose `BIFROST_API_KEY` client-side.
 
 - `CREEM_API_KEY` — Creem API key for server-side checkout creation.
 - `CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — Secret used to verify Creem webhook signatures.
 - `CREEM_PRODUCT_ID_DIO_CREDITS` — The configured Creem product ID for Dio credits.
 - `DIO_CREDIT_BUDGET` — Bifrost virtual-key budget (credits) assigned to each purchase.
 - `BIFROST_API_KEY` — Server-side Bifrost API key. In local dev, set via `export BIFROST_API_KEY="$(fnox get bifrost_api_key)"`.
+- `BIFROST_BASE_URL` — Bifrost gateway URL (default: `https://bifrost.dio.computer`).
+- `BIFROST_MODEL_ALIAS` — Model allowed for the provisioned virtual key (default: `kimi-k2.6`).
+- `RESEND_API_KEY` — Resend API key for sending recovery OTP emails.
+- `DIO_RECOVERY_FROM_EMAIL` — Sender address for recovery emails (default: `noreply@keating.io`).
+- `DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE` — When `true`, recovery OTPs are logged and returned instead of emailed (default: `true` when `NODE_ENV=development`).
 
 ## Important Files & Their Roles
 
 | File | Purpose |
 |------|---------|
 | `keating.config.json` | Per-project runtime/model config. Gitignored in projects. |
 | `chrysalis.config.json` | Repo-local config for Chrysalis/Pi tooling. |
 | `SYSTEM.md` | System prompt sent to LLMs when Keating acts as a tutor. |
 | `src/core/topics.ts` | Hardcoded topic definitions + domain keyword heuristics for fallback generation. |
 | `src/core/policy.ts` | Default policy, clamping, signature hashing. |
 | `src/core/project.ts` | Central artifact coordinator. All CLI and Pi commands funnel through here. |
 | `src/core/animation.ts` | Manim-web bundle generation. Only core file that calls LLM. |
 | `src/core/benchmark.ts` | Synthetic learner suite. Deterministic unless using real LLM judge mode. |
 | `src/core/prompt-evolution.ts` | Prompt scoring + PROSPER selection. |
 | `web/src/hooks/useKeatingAgent.tsx` | Web app state machine (Agent, sessions, speech, storage). |
 | `web/src/keating/browser-tools.ts` | Web-ported tool definitions matching CLI capabilities. |
 | `test/helpers.ts` | Deterministic benchmark stubs + property arbitraries + invariant helpers. |
 
 ## What NOT to Do
 
 - Do not add a new topic without checking if domain-specific phase injections are appropriate (see `lesson-plan.ts`).
 - Do not make benchmark or evolution code depend on LLM calls in tests — the test suite expects deterministic stubs.
 - Do not change `pi.registerCommand()` handler signatures without updating `src/core/commands.ts` which generates command help text.
 - Do not assume the web build is just `vite build` — it is `vite build && npx nitro build`.
 - Do not commit generated artifacts — `.keating/`, `dist/`, `web/dist/`, `web/.output/` are all gitignored.

parts.text: Now update the env config test to include new optional fields.

Now update the recovery test. The current test creates challenge separately and expects requiresOtp. I need to test dev code returned. Also the test uses `createRecoveryChallenge(createMemoryStorage(), ...)` which is fine.

But with the new code, recover.ts calls `getDioEnvConfig()` which in dev mode returns recoveryDevCode=true. In test mode (NODE_ENV=test), we need to set DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE=true for dev code.

Let me update the recovery tests.

Now add tests for event lock and provisioning. I need to add new tests and update existing ones.

The existing test "ignores duplicate event ids" passes with the new lock because our fake storage's setItem doesn't support nx, but the read-verify logic should still work for sequential calls. However, in single-threaded test, the second call reads the existing processing record and returns false. Good.

But I should add tests for:
- Event lock stale recovery (old processing record can be re-acquired)
- Failed event can be retried (already covered by existing logic? No test for it)
- Concurrent lock acquisition (simulate with Map storage?)

Also update the webhook test to verify idempotency header and provisioning record.

Let me add new tests after existing webhook tests. First, let me read the updated test file to position correctly.

metadata.preview:
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`

Keating tries the configured provider first, then falls back to OpenAI/Anthropic if the default key is missing.

### Web / Dio provider

Used by the web app's Dio credit purchase and Bifrost virtual-key provisioning routes. Never expose `BIFROST_API_KEY` client-side.

- `CREEM_API_KEY` — Creem API key for server-side checkout creation.
- `CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — Secret used to verify Creem webhook signatures.
- `CREEM_PRODUCT_ID_DIO_CREDITS` — The configured Creem product ID for Dio credits.
- `DIO_CREDIT_BUDGET` — Bifrost virtual-key budget (credits) assigned to each purchase.
- `BIFROST_API_KEY` — Server-side Bifrost API key. In local dev, set via `export BIFROST_API_KEY="$(fnox get bifrost_api_key)"`.

## Important Files & Their Roles

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `keating.config.json` | Per-project runtime/model config. Gitignored in projects. |
| `chrysalis.config.json` | Repo-local config for Chrysalis/Pi tooling. |

display.text:
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`

Keating tries the configured provider first, then falls back to OpenAI/Anthropic if the default key is missing.

### Web / Dio provider

Used by the web app's Dio credit purchase and Bifrost virtual-key provisioning routes. Never expose `BIFROST_API_KEY` client-side.

- `CREEM_API_KEY` — Creem API key for server-side checkout creation.
- `CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — Secret used to verify Creem webhook signatures.
- `CREEM_PRODUCT_ID_DIO_CREDITS` — The configured Creem product ID for Dio credits.
- `DIO_CREDIT_BUDGET` — Bifrost virtual-key budget (credits) assigned to each purchase.
- `BIFROST_API_KEY` — Server-side Bifrost API key. In local dev, set via `export BIFROST_API_KEY="$(fnox get bifrost_api_key)"`.

## Important Files & Their Roles

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `keating.config.json` | Per-project runtime/model config. Gitignored in projects. |
| `chrysalis.config.json` | Repo-local config for Chrysalis/Pi tooling. |
| `SYSTEM.md` | System prompt sent to LLMs when Keating acts as a tutor. |
| `src/core/topics.ts` | Hardcoded topic definitions + domain keyword heuristics for fallback generation. |
| `src/core/policy.ts` | Default policy, clamping, signature hashing. |
| `src/core/project.ts` | Central artifact coordinator. All CLI and Pi commands funnel through here. |
| `src/core/animation.ts` | Manim-web bundle generation. Only core file that calls LLM. |

input.newString:
### Web / Dio provider

Used by the web app's Dio credit purchase and Bifrost virtual-key provisioning routes. Never expose `BIFROST_API_KEY` client-side.

- `CREEM_API_KEY` — Creem API key for server-side checkout creation.
- `CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — Secret used to verify Creem webhook signatures.
- `CREEM_PRODUCT_ID_DIO_CREDITS` — The configured Creem product ID for Dio credits.
- `DIO_CREDIT_BUDGET` — Bifrost virtual-key budget (credits) assigned to each purchase.
- `BIFROST_API_KEY` — Server-side Bifrost API key. In local dev, set via `export BIFROST_API_KEY="$(fnox get bifrost_api_key)"`.
- `BIFROST_BASE_URL` — Bifrost gateway URL (default: `https://bifrost.dio.computer`).
- `BIFROST_MODEL_ALIAS` — Model allowed for the provisioned virtual key (default: `kimi-k2.6`).
- `RESEND_API_KEY` — Resend API key for sending recovery OTP emails.
- `DIO_RECOVERY_FROM_EMAIL` — Sender address for recovery emails (default: `noreply@keating.io`).
- `DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE` — When `true`, recovery OTPs are logged and returned instead of emailed (default: `true` when `NODE_ENV=development`).

@@ -226,6 +226,21 @@ keating feedback up|down|confused [topic] [--comment=...]
 
 Keating tries the configured provider first, then falls back to OpenAI/Anthropic if the default key is missing.
 
+### Web / Dio provider
+
+Used by the web app's Dio credit purchase and Bifrost virtual-key provisioning routes. Never expose `BIFROST_API_KEY` client-side.
+
+- `CREEM_API_KEY` — Creem API key for server-side checkout creation.
+- `CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` — Secret used to verify Creem webhook signatures.
+- `CREEM_PRODUCT_ID_DIO_CREDITS` — The configured Creem product ID for Dio credits.
+- `DIO_CREDIT_BUDGET` — Bifrost virtual-key budget (credits) assigned to each purchase.
+- `BIFROST_API_KEY` — Server-side Bifrost API key. In local dev, set via `export BIFROST_API_KEY="$(fnox get bifrost_api_key)"`.
+- `BIFROST_BASE_URL` — Bifrost gateway URL (default: `https://bifrost.dio.computer`).
+- `BIFROST_MODEL_ALIAS` — Model allowed for the provisioned virtual key (default: `kimi-k2.6`).
+- `RESEND_API_KEY` — Resend API key for sending recovery OTP emails.
+- `DIO_RECOVERY_FROM_EMAIL` — Sender address for recovery emails (default: `noreply@keating.io`).
+- `DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE` — When `true`, recovery OTPs are logged and returned instead of emailed (default: `true` when `NODE_ENV=development`).
+
 ## Important Files & Their Roles
 
 | File | Purpose |

  Findings

  - High: web typecheck fails. web/server/api/dio/webhook.ts:59 passes Nitro Storage into web/src/dio-provider/server.ts:393, whose setItem type uses unknown and is incompatible with Nitro StorageValue. web/test/dio-

  - High: Recovery can still become non-functional in production. web/src/dio-provider/server.ts:324 swallows Resend failures and falls through; if dev codes are disabled it returns { sent: false }, but web/server/api/dio/
    recover.ts:38 still tells the client OTP is required. The user never receives a code.

  - Medium: Event locking is improved but not proven atomic. web/src/dio-provider/server.ts:417 attempts { nx: true }, but correctness depends on the active Nitro storage driver supporting that option. If it ignores it, the
    read-back check is not a distributed lock guarantee.

  Fixed/Improved
  The email-only key leak is fixed for claim/recover, /dio/success now exists, stale event locks have a TTL, and provisioning records plus Bifrost idempotency keys reduce duplicate-key risk.

state.title: Run web TypeScript type check

parts.text:
More type errors. The test's `createMemoryStorage` returns a partial object not matching `Storage`. And the emailPayload optional chaining gives type 'never' because of how I typed it? Hmm.

I need to either:
1. Change server.ts storage types back to a structural type that's compatible with both Nitro Storage and partial test fakes.
2. Make test fakes satisfy Storage type (too many methods).
3. Use a custom minimal storage interface.

Better approach: define a minimal `DioStorage` interface in server.ts that captures only what we need, and use it. Nitro Storage should be assignable to it.

```ts
export interface DioStorage {
  getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>;
  setItem: (key: string, value: unknown, opts?: unknown) => Promise<void>;
  hasItem?: (key: string) => Promise<boolean>;
  removeItem?: (key: string) => Promise<void>;
}
```

Wait, can I define `setItem` with `value: unknown` and have Nitro's `setItem(value: StorageValue)` be assignable to it? Due to contravariance, no. A function accepting `unknown` is NOT assignable to a function accepting `StorageValue`. Actually, we need the OPPOSITE: we want to pass Nitro Storage (with setItem(value: StorageValue)) to a parameter expecting setItem(value: unknown). 

Parameter type `unknown` is wider than `StorageValue`. A function expecting `unknown` can be called with `StorageValue`. So `setItem(value: unknown)` is assignable to `setItem(value: StorageValue)`? No.

Function assignability is contravariant in parameter types. If `F1 = (x: A) => R` and `F2 = (x: B) => R`, then `F1` is assignable to `F2` if `B` is assignable to `A`. 

Here, Nitro's setItem is `(key: string, value: StorageValue, opts?: TransactionOptions) => Promise<void>`. Our parameter expects `(key: string, value: unknown, opts?: unknown) => Promise<void>`.

For Nitro's setItem to be assignable to our parameter type, the parameter type of our expected function must be assignable to Nitro's parameter type. `unknown` is assignable to `StorageValue`? No, `unknown` is not assignable to `StorageValue` because `StorageValue` is narrower.

Wait, function parameter contravariance: `(x: A) => R` assignable to `(x: B) => R` iff `B extends A`.

Expected parameter type: `(key: string, value: unknown) => ...` means A = unknown.
Actual parameter type (Nitro): `(key: string, value: StorageValue) => ...` means B = StorageValue.
Is B assignable to A? Is StorageValue assignable to unknown? Yes, everything is assignable to unknown.

So Nitro's setItem SHOULD be assignable to our expected setItem. But the error says it's not because of `value: unknown` is not assignable to `StorageValue`. That's the other direction.

Wait, re-reading error: "Argument of type 'Storage<StorageValue>' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Storage'". That's because I changed the parameter to `Storage` (unstorage). And test fake is not `Storage`.

The original error was: webhook.ts Storage not assignable to `{ getItem/setItem }`. Because the structural type had `setItem(value: unknown)` and Nitro's setItem has `value: StorageValue`. Contravariance issue.

So to fix the original error, I need the parameter type's setItem to accept `StorageValue` or wider. But our functions want to call setItem with `unknown` values? Actually no, we pass objects that are `StorageValue`. So our parameter type's setItem can accept `StorageValue`.

Better: make the DioStorage interface use `value: any` or `value: StorageValue`. If `value: any`, Nitro's setItem(value: StorageValue) is assignable to setItem(value: any)? Parameter type `any` is assignable to `StorageValue`, so yes.

Actually, using `any` for the value parameter makes it bivariant-ish. Let me try:

```ts
export interface DioStorage {
  getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>;
  setItem: (key: string, value: any, opts?: any) => Promise<void>;
}
```

Nitro Storage's setItem has `value: StorageValue`. Is `StorageValue` assignable to `any`? Yes, everything is assignable to any. And is `(value: any) => void` assignable to `(value: StorageValue) => void`? Parameter type `StorageValue` extends `any`, so yes. 

Actually, `any` disables strictness. Using `any` is acceptable here for a generic storage abstraction.

Alternatively, use `value: StorageValue` from unstorage. But then test fake setItem with `value: unknown` wouldn't be assignable. Hmm.

Best: define our own `DioStorage` with `getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>` and `setItem: (key: string, value: unknown, opts?: unknown) => Promise<void>`. But this has the contravariance problem with Nitro.

Wait, I need to carefully think about which direction.

We have function F_param expected by our code: `setItem(key: string, value: SomeType, opts?: SomeOpt) => Promise<void>`.
We pass a concrete object with setItem: `setItem(key: string, value: StorageValue, opts?: TransactionOptions) => Promise<void>` (Nitro).

For the concrete object to be assignable to the parameter type, every property must be assignable. So Nitro's setItem function must be assignable to our parameter's setItem function type.

Function assignability: `(x: B) => R` assignable to `(x: A) => R` iff `A` assignable to `B`.

Our parameter setItem value type A = SomeType.
Nitro setItem value type B = StorageValue.

We need `SomeType` assignable to `StorageValue`.

If SomeType = unknown, is unknown assignable to StorageValue? NO. That's why it failed.

If SomeType = StorageValue, is StorageValue assignable to StorageValue? YES.

If SomeType = any, is any assignable to StorageValue? YES (in TypeScript, any is assignable to anything).

So using `any` or `StorageValue` works for Nitro. But tests use `value: unknown` in fake setItem. For test fake to be assignable to parameter type:

Test fake setItem value type B = unknown.
Parameter setItem value type A = SomeType.
Need `SomeType` assignable to `unknown`? Wait, function assignability direction: `(x: B) => R` assignable to `(x: A) => R` iff `A` assignable to `B`.

So if parameter A = StorageValue, need StorageValue assignable to unknown? No, StorageValue is not assignable to unknown. So test fake with `value: unknown` not assignable to parameter expecting `value: StorageValue`.

If parameter A = any, need any assignable to unknown? any is assignable to anything, so yes.

If parameter A = unknown, need unknown assignable to unknown? yes. But then Nitro fails.

So the only type that works for both Nitro and test fake is `any` (or a union that includes both, but no union type is broader than unknown and also assignable to StorageValue).

Actually, I could type the test fake setItem as `value: any` too. Or type parameter as `value: any`.

Using `any` is the pragmatic solution.

Let me define:
```ts
export interface DioStorage {
  getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>;
  setItem: (key: string, value: any, opts?: any) => Promise<void>;
}
```

And use `DioStorage` instead of `Storage` in all function signatures. This avoids importing unstorage too.

But the original import of `Storage` from unstorage is only for type. If I define my own interface, I can remove the import.

Let me do this.

Also, the emailPayload type issue: I typed it as `{ to?: string; from?: string; subject?: string } | null`. But in the current code, `emailPayload = JSON.parse(...)` returns `any`. TypeScript infers from the assignment target. If target is `{ to?: string; ... } | null`, then after `not.toBeNull()` assertion, it should narrow to `{ to?: string; ... }`. But the error says `Property 'to' does not exist on type 'never'`.

Why never? Because optional chaining on a null-only type? Let me check. `emailPayload` starts as `null` (initial value). Then assigned `JSON.parse(...)` which is typed based on the variable type. Hmm, maybe TypeScript narrows emailPayload to `null` through control flow and then after `not.toBeNull()` it becomes `never`? That would mean TypeScript thinks emailPayload is always null. 

Wait, `emailPayload` is reassigned inside the fetch mock callback. TypeScript might not see that. It could think it remains null. Then `expect(emailPayload).not.toBeNull()` narrows `null` by excluding null → never. That's why `.to` doesn't exist on never.

To fix, I can cast at the expect site or use a type predicate. Simpler: make `emailPayload` type `Record<string, unknown>` and use `(emailPayload as Record<string, unknown>).to`? But that was the original issue - null cast.

Wait, the original issue was casting `emailPayload` (which was `Record<string, unknown> | null`) to `Record<string, unknown>` when it might be null. TypeScript error: "Conversion of type 'null' to type 'Record<string, unknown>' may be a mistake..."

So the real fix is to first assert it's not null, then access. But TypeScript doesn't understand `expect().not.toBeNull()` narrowing because Bun's expect types might not narrow.

I can use a runtime check:
```ts
expect(emailPayload).not.toBeNull();
if (emailPayload) {
  expect(emailPayload.to).toBe(...);
  expect(emailPayload.from).toBe(...);
}
```

But inside the if block, since emailPayload was `Record<string, unknown> | null` originally, it narrows to `Record<string, unknown>`, but still `.to` doesn't exist on Record<string, unknown>. 

So I need emailPayload type to have `to` property. But then null narrowing issue.

Best solution: use `expect(emailPayload).toBeTruthy()`? No, same issue.

Use a type assertion with unknown first:
```ts
expect(emailPayload).not.toBeNull();
const payload = emailPayload as unknown as Record<string, unknown>;
expect(payload.to).toBe(...);
```

But the original error was direct null cast. The `as unknown as Record` is the standard way. However, the finding says "invalid null casts". Using unknown intermediate is valid.

Actually, the cleanest: don't use null union. Initialize with `{}` or use a truthy check.

Let me change emailPayload to be non-null:
```ts
let emailPayload: { to?: string; from?: string } = { to: "", from: "" };
```

Then after fetch, it's set. Then expect works. But we want to verify it was set. We can check `emailPayload.to !== undefined` or just expect.

Actually, simpler:
```ts
let emailPayload: Record<string, string> = {};
...
expect(emailPayload.to).toBe("buyer@example.com");
expect(emailPayload.from).toBe("recovery@keating.test");
```

But TypeScript still complains because `Record<string, string>` doesn't have `.to` property. Hmm, yes it does have index signature, but not named property. Accessing `.to` on `Record<string, string>` is allowed? I think it returns `string | undefined`. No, Record<string, string> has index signature `[x: string]: string`, so `.to` is allowed but returns `string` (not undefined).

Actually, in TypeScript, if a type has an index signature, property access returns the index type. So `emailPayload.to` is `string`. But `emailPayload` is `Record<string, string>`, which means all string keys map to string. So `.to` is string. This should work.

But if initialized with `{}`, it's empty but the type says no undefined. Better to use `Partial<Record<string, string>>` or `Record<string, string | undefined>`.

Actually, the simplest valid type: `Record<string, unknown>` and use bracket notation:
```ts
expect(emailPayload["to"]).toBe("buyer@example.com");
expect(emailPayload["from"]).toBe("recovery@keating.test");
```

Wait, `Record<string, unknown>` has index signature `[x: string]: unknown`, so `emailPayload["to"]` is `unknown`. `expect(unknown).toBe(...)` should work because expect accepts unknown.

But the original code used dot notation. Bracket notation with Record<string, unknown> is fine.

Actually, the TypeScript issue with optional chaining was that `emailPayload` was narrowed to `never`. Let me just use bracket notation on a non-null `Record<string, unknown>`:

```ts
let emailPayload: Record<string, unknown> = {};
...
expect(emailPayload["to"]).toBe("buyer@example.com");
expect(emailPayload["from"]).toBe("recovery@keating.test");
```

This avoids the null cast issue. The initial value `{}` is valid for `Record<string, unknown>`.

Wait, `Record<string, unknown>` usually means an object with index signature. `{}` assignable? I think yes.

OK let me just revert my emailPayload type change and use bracket notation.

Actually, the original error was specifically about casting null to Record. So if I don't cast null and use bracket notation, it's fine.

Let me update.

input.newString: let emailPayload: Record<string, unknown> = {};

But wait, the comment above `acquireEventLock` says "The underlying storage write attempts an atomic set-if-not-exists..." and might reference Storage. Let me check if replaceAll accidentally changed something unintended. Also `DioStorage` type definition says `getItem: (key: string) => Promise<unknown>; setItem: (key: string, value: any, opts?: any) => Promise<void>;` - this should be fine.

But I just replaced `Storage` with `DioStorage` everywhere, including the interface name itself? Let me check. `export interface DioStorage` was defined, then replaceAll changed any `Storage` to `DioStorage`. But the interface definition itself already has `DioStorage`. There shouldn't be bare `Storage` elsewhere. Let me grep.

Good, no bare Storage. But I need to check if type casts or comments were affected. Actually the grep found none, so the only `Storage` occurrences were the ones I intended. The import was removed.

Wait, but I had `import type { Storage } from "unstorage";` and replaced it. Then I added `DioStorage` interface. The replaceAll didn't change the interface name because it was already DioStorage. Good.

Now I need to make sure the test fake storage satisfies DioStorage. The test's `createMemoryStorage()` has:
```ts
getItem: async (key: string) => storage.get(key) ?? null,
setItem: async (key: string, value: unknown) => { storage.set(key, value); },
```

`getItem` returns `Promise<{} | null>` which is assignable to `Promise<unknown>`. 
`setItem` has `value: unknown`. DioStorage expects `value: any`. Is `(value: unknown) => void` assignable to `(value: any) => void`? Yes, because any is assignable to unknown? Wait:

Parameter of DioStorage setItem: `any`.
Parameter of test fake setItem: `unknown`.
Function assignability: `(x: B) => R` assignable to `(x: A) => R` iff `A` assignable to `B`.
A = any, B = unknown. Is any assignable to unknown? Yes, any is assignable to anything.

So test fake setItem is assignable to DioStorage setItem. Good.

Now the racingStorage in the concurrent test:
```ts
const racingStorage = {
  getItem: async (key: string) => shared.get(key) ?? null,
  setItem: async (key: string, value: unknown, _opts?: unknown) => { shared.set(key, value); },
};
```

Same as above, should be assignable.

But earlier typecheck error said racingStorage was missing properties. That was when parameter type was `Storage` from unstorage. Now with `DioStorage`, it only needs getItem/setItem. Should be fine.

There's also `createMemoryStorage()` used in recovery test with `createRecoveryChallenge(createMemoryStorage(), ...)`. That should be fine.

Now run typecheck again.

parts.text:
The test's createMemoryStorage has `removeItem: async (key: string) => storage.delete(key)` which returns `Promise<boolean>` because Map.delete returns boolean. But DioStorage.removeItem expects `Promise<void>`.

I can either:
1. Update DioStorage.removeItem to return `Promise<unknown>` or `Promise<any>`.
2. Update test createMemoryStorage removeItem to not return.

Option 1 is simpler - make optional methods more flexible. Or remove removeItem from DioStorage since we don't use it. But the test includes it. The simplest is to change DioStorage interface to not require exact return type for optional methods, or just remove the optional methods entirely (hasItem, removeItem) since our code doesn't use them. But then the test object still has extra properties - which is fine because structural typing allows extra properties.

Actually, the issue is the test fake has `removeItem` property with type `Promise<boolean>`, and DioStorage expects `removeItem?: (key: string) => Promise<void>`. For object assignability, extra properties are allowed, but properties with matching names must be assignable. So `removeItem: Promise<boolean>` must be assignable to `removeItem?: Promise<void>`. It's not because boolean not assignable to void.

Solution: remove `hasItem` and `removeItem` from DioStorage interface (we don't use them). Then the test fake's extra properties are allowed without type checking.

But wait, the test fake is passed to functions expecting DioStorage. If DioStorage only has getItem/setItem, then the fake object has extra hasItem/removeItem, which is fine.

Let me remove the optional methods from DioStorage.
Run typecheck again.

The new test failed. The handler resolved instead of rejecting. Why?

Because `sendRecoveryEmail` in recover.ts calls `getDioEnvConfig()` which is evaluated when? In the function call. The handler imports `getDioEnvConfig` already. But maybe process.env values weren't set properly because import is cached and old config is used?

Wait, `recover.ts` imports getDioEnvConfig from server.ts. When the test imports the recover handler, it also imports server.ts. The first time server.ts is loaded (in the test's module), process.env has the values set in beforeEach. So getDioEnvConfig should return recoveryDevCode=false.

But maybe the issue is that the recovered handler was imported earlier in another test with DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE=true, and the module is cached. In Node/Bun, ES modules are cached. So getDioEnvConfig function exists, but when called in the test, it reads current process.env. So should be fine.

Wait, the error shows "Failed to send recovery email to buyer@example.com: Resend is down" which means sendRecoveryEmail was called and the catch block ran. Also it says "in production (dev codes disabled), a provider failure must surface as an error" and returns `{ sent: false, error: message }`. 

Then recover.ts checks `emailResult.error` and throws 503. But the handler resolved. That means `emailResult.error` was falsy? No, it should have error.

Wait, maybe `getDioEnvConfig()` throws because DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE is not set and NODE_ENV might be "test"? Let me check: in beforeEach, NODE_ENV is restored from envBackup. In the first test, envBackup for NODE_ENV is whatever it was. The test sets process.env.NODE_ENV? No, beforeEach just backs it up. It doesn't set it. So NODE_ENV could be undefined or whatever the environment has.

In my test, I set `process.env.DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE = "false"` and `process.env.RESEND_API_KEY = "resend_test_key"`. But I don't set NODE_ENV. getDioEnvConfig: `recoveryDevCode: process.env.DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE === "true" || process.env.NODE_ENV === "development"`. With DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE="false", recoveryDevCode is false. Good.

So why did handler resolve? Maybe the createError didn't throw? Let me check. `throw createError({ statusCode: 503, statusMessage: emailResult.error });` should throw.

Maybe emailResult.error was empty? The Resend response body has `{ message: "Resend is down" }`. In sendRecoveryEmail catch, `body?.message` is "Resend is down". So error is set.

Wait, maybe the handler caught the error and returned a success object? No, recover.ts doesn't have try/catch around the send.

Let me re-read recover.ts.

state.title: Run root test suite

parts.text:
Root tests pass.

Now let me review the final state of changes. Let me update todo list and prepare final summary.

Actually, let me also check if there's any issue with the event lock mutex. The `eventLockPromises` map could grow unbounded if errors occur, but I clean up in finally. Good.

One concern: `await previous` in acquireEventLock will wait even if previous returned false. That's fine - serializes callers.

Another concern: the mutex prevents concurrent lock attempts in the same process. Combined with nx+read-back, this is strong. For multi-instance, still relies on storage backend.

I think we've addressed the findings. Let me review the updated server.ts section for event lock and sendRecoveryEmail to make sure they're correct.

Actually, one more thing about sendRecoveryEmail: in the catch block, I log error and then if !recoveryDevCode return error. But what if recoveryDevCode is true? It falls through to the dev code return. But the OTP was already generated. The dev code return gives the same OTP. Good.

But should the email challenge still be saved if email fails? Yes, it's saved before sendRecoveryEmail in createRecoveryChallenge. The user can retry recovery and a new OTP is generated.

Actually, wait: in recover.ts, if emailResult.error, I throw 503. But the challenge was already saved. Next request creates a new challenge. Fine.

Let me also verify the final code doesn't have any obvious issues. Read server.ts event lock and recovery sections.

Also, I should check test file doesn't have type errors now (already verified).

Let me update todo list.

Make recovery fail loudly when email cannot be sent in production
Strengthen event lock with same-process mutex and document distributed guarantee

parts.text:
Fixed the remaining issues:

- **Typecheck**: Introduced a small `DioStorage` interface (`getItem`/`setItem` with `any` value) in `web/src/dio-provider/server.ts` so both Nitro Storage and test fakes are assignable. Removed the invalid `null` casts in `web/test/dio-server.test.ts` by using bracket access on a non-null `Record<string, unknown>`.

- **Recovery failure handling**: `sendRecoveryEmail` now returns an `error` when a configured Resend provider fails and dev codes are disabled, and when no provider is configured at all. `web/server/api/dio/recover.ts` checks `emailResult.error` and returns HTTP 503 instead of silently asking for an OTP the user will never receive.

- **Event locking**: Added an in-process promise queue (`eventLockPromises`) so concurrent handlers for the same event in the same Node process serialize before hitting storage. The `nx: true` attempt + lock-id re-read remains for cross-process safety; the doc comment now explicitly notes that multi-instance hard guarantees depend on the storage backend honoring `nx` or providing linearizable writes.

Verification:
- `flox activate -- bunx tsc -p web/tsconfig.json --noEmit` → clean
- `flox activate -- just test-web` → 226 pass, 0 fail
- `flox activate -- just test` → 231 pass, 0 fail
- `flox activate -- just build-all` → succeeds
okay so onto deploy meant i want to know how i would deploy this now

To deploy this you'll need to push the changes to a Git host and trigger a Railway deployment. Since these files are currently untracked, the first step is to commit them. Do you want me to stage and commit the Dio provider changes, or would you prefer to review the diff first?

Also, what's your target?

- **Railway** (the project already uses Railway tooling in the available functions)
- **Self-hosted / VPS**
- **Something else**

And are you deploying:
- Just the web app (`web/`)
- The CLI/Node package (`keating` on npm)
- Both
what about the env for the site

Environment variables for the deployed site depend on where you deploy. For Dio credit purchases and recovery emails, the **server-side-only** env vars must be set in the hosting environment, not in the client bundle.

Required for Dio to work:

```bash
CREEM_API_KEY=
CREEM_WEBHOOK_SECRET=
CREEM_PRODUCT_ID_DIO_CREDITS=
DIO_CREDIT_BUDGET=500
BIFROST_API_KEY=
RESEND_API_KEY=          # for recovery emails
DIO_RECOVERY_FROM_EMAIL= # default: noreply@keating.io
```

Optional:

```bash
BIFROST_BASE_URL=https://bifrost.dio.computer
BIFROST_MODEL_ALIAS=kimi-k2.6
DIO_RECOVERY_DEV_CODE=false
KEATING_WEB_ORIGIN=      # used for Creem success_url callback
```

Client-side (`VITE_*`) vars go in `web/.env` or the frontend build env:

```bash
VITE_POSTHOG_PROJECT_TOKEN=
VITE_POSTHOG_HOST=https://us.i.posthog.com
VITE_POSTHOG_DISABLED=false
```

If you're deploying to Railway, I can set these variables on the service from the CLI. Do you want me to:

1. Check the current Railway project/service for this repo
2. Set the variables interactively (you'd paste in the secret values)
3. Or deploy first and then set them?
deploy with railway and set these vars up, but also i want you to gate this with a feature flag
I'll deploy to Railway and gate Dio behind a feature flag. First, let me check the current Railway project/service and recent deployments, then I'll implement the flag.

Let me plan feature flag. Two approaches:
- Server-side: new env var `DIO_ENABLED=false` (or `DIO_FEATURE_FLAG`). When false, API routes return 404 or disabled, UI doesn't show Dio option.
- Client-side: `VITE_DIO_ENABLED` to hide UI. But server must also reject.

Best: use a server-side env var and expose it to client via an API route or build-time VITE var. Simplest: set `VITE_DIO_ENABLED` for UI and `DIO_ENABLED` for server. But keeping them in sync is error-prone.

Alternatively, server-side only: UI can fetch config from `/api/dio/config` to know if enabled. But that's an extra request.

Simpler: use `VITE_DIO_ENABLED` for both. In server routes, also read it. But server env vars without VITE_ prefix are also available at runtime in Nitro. We can just use `DIO_ENABLED` on server and `VITE_DIO_ENABLED` on client. Or use `VITE_DIO_ENABLED` everywhere if we set it.

Actually, the cleanest is to have a single env var `DIO_ENABLED` and expose whether Dio is enabled through the existing provider/API. But for the UI prompt, we want to know at runtime without extra fetch. 

We can add a server API `/api/dio/status` that returns `{ enabled: boolean }`. The UI fetches this when considering Dio. Or we can set `VITE_DIO_ENABLED` at build time.
